# Week 06 - Lecture 01

## Rough Agenda

### **4:05 pm - 5:55 pm** 

+ Introducing Random Effects (@sec-randomeff)
+ Distributions (@sec-distributions)
+ Introduction to GLMs (@sec-glms)

::: {.callout-tip}
## By the end of today's seminar, you should be able to
 + Fit and critique linear mixed effects models
 + Discuss, describe & critique different probability distributions 
 + Appropriately map a random variable to a (likely) distribution
:::


## Introducing random effcets {#sec-randomeff}

**Slides** - *code along*

```{r, eval = FALSE}
base_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/STATS-UOA/databunker/master/data/"
rcbd <- readr::read_csv(paste(base_url, "rcbd.csv", sep = ""))
rcbd$Run <- as.factor(rcbd$Run)
lm <- lm(logAUC8 ~ Run + Surgery, data = rcbd)
lm |> summary()
anova(lm)
lmer4_mod <- lme4::lmer(logAUC8 ~ Surgery + (1|Run), data = rcbd)
coefficients(lmer4_mod)
lmer4_mod |> summary()
```




<p align="center">
<iframe src="slides/05-04_fixed-vs-random.pdf" height="400" width="700" title="Fixed vs Random effects slides"></iframe>
</p>
<p align="center">
</p>


### Case studies 

As a group, work though each of the case studies given below answering/discussing the prompts as you go. You'll notice a few extra packages and functions getting used :-)



#### Case study 01

Visiting [this section of the course guide](https://biosci738.github.io/BIOSCI738/linear-mixed-effect-models-lmms.html) may be useful.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
require(tidyverse)
require(lme4)
require(predictmeans)
rcbd <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/STATS-UOA/databunker/master/data/rcbd.csv")
## turn appropriate variables into factors
rcbd <- rcbd %>%
  mutate(Run = as.factor(Run)) %>%
  mutate(Surgery = as.factor(Surgery))
## run as a fixed effect
lm(logAUC8 ~ Run + Surgery, data = rcbd) |> summary()
## run as a random effect, what's the difference?
lmm <- lme4::lmer(logAUC8 ~ Surgery + (1|Run), data = rcbd) 
summary(lmm)
## diagnostics estimated variance partitioning and more...
## these are really useful utility functions!
predictmeans::residplot(lmm)
predictmeans::R2_glmm(lmm)
predictmeans::se_ranef(lmm)
predictmeans::varcomp(lmm)
predictmeans::permmodels(lmm) ## remember permutation tests, what are we using them for here? Compare to a summary() output

```

#### Case study 02

Now, we're going to get a little more advanced and model some data from @monkey (data retrieved from @monkeydata). To do so we're following along (somewhat) with the steps summarised in @danblog. Revisiting @sec-randomeff may, again, be useful here.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
## wrangle as per Dan's blog
require(tidyverse)
data <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/STATS-UOA/databunker/master/data/monkey.csv")
data %>%
    ggplot(., aes(x = age, y = Activity)) +
    geom_point() 
## Number of active intervals in the first two minutes (0-8)
activity_2mins <- data |>
  filter(obs<9) |> group_by(subj_id, Day) |>
  summarize(total=sum(Activity), 
            active_bins = sum(Activity > 0), 
            age = min(age)) |>
  rename(monkey = subj_id, day = Day) |>
ungroup()
length(table(activity_2mins$monkey))
activity_2mins %>%
    ggplot(., aes(x = age, y = total, col = as.factor(monkey))) +
    facet_wrap(~day) +
    geom_point() + theme(legend.position = "none")
## Linear model...
fit_lm <- lm(active_bins ~ age*factor(day) + factor(monkey), data = activity_2mins)
fit_lm |>
summary()
## ignore monkeys
fit_lm_pool <- lm(active_bins ~ age*factor(day), data = activity_2mins)
fit_lm_pool |>
summary()
fit_lm_pool |> gglm::gglm() ## and what do we think here?
## plot
plot(fit_lm_pool$fitted, fit_lm_pool$residuals)
## why scale?
age_centre <- mean(activity_2mins$age)
age_scale <- diff(range(activity_2mins$age))/2
active_bins_centre <- 4

activity_2mins_scaled <- activity_2mins |>
  mutate(monkey = factor(monkey),
         day = factor(day),
         age_centred = (age - age_centre)/age_scale,
         active_bins_scaled = (active_bins - active_bins_centre)/4)
glimpse(activity_2mins_scaled)

## Monkey as a random effect, why? Is this sensible?
aov(active_bins_scaled ~ age_centred*day + Error(monkey), data = activity_2mins_scaled) |>
summary()
## formula
formula <- active_bins_scaled ~ age_centred*day + (1 | monkey)
## lme4
library(lme4)
fit_lme4 <- lme4::lmer(formula, activity_2mins_scaled)
fit_lme4 |>
summary()
predictmeans::predictmeans(fit_lme4, "day")
## lmerTest, is this the same model as above
library(lmerTest)
fit_lmerTest <- lmerTest::lmer(formula, activity_2mins_scaled)
fit_lmerTest |>
summary()
predictmeans::predictmeans(fit_lmerTest, "day")
## glmmTMB, is this the same model as above
library(glmmTMB)
fit_glmmtmb <- glmmTMB::glmmTMB(formula, activity_2mins_scaled)
fit_glmmtmb |> summary()
## and what does this show?
emmeans::emmeans(fit_glmmtmb, specs = "day") |>
 plot(pairwise = TRUE)
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Mixed effects model syntax quiz

Select the correct model formula!

<style>
.quiz-question {
  margin-bottom: 30px;
}

.quiz-option {
  display: block;
  padding: 8px;
  margin: 5px 0;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 6px;
  cursor: pointer;
}

.correct {
  background-color: #c8f7c5;
}

.incorrect {
  background-color: #f7c5c5;
}
</style>

<script>
function checkAnswer(el, correct) {
  let options = el.parentElement.children;
  for (let opt of options) {
    opt.classList.remove("correct", "incorrect");
  }
  
  if (correct) {
    el.classList.add("correct");
  } else {
    el.classList.add("incorrect");
  }
}
</script>


### Question 1

You measure **logAUC8** for patients.  
You want to test the effect of **Surgery**.  
Measurements were taken across different **Runs**.

Treat **Run** as a random effect.

<div class="quiz-question">
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">logAUC8 ~ Surgery + (1|Run)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">logAUC8 ~ Surgery + Run</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">logAUC8 ~ Surgery + (Run|1)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">logAUC8 ~ Surgery + (1 + Run)</div>
</div>



### Question 2

You measure **Yield**.  
You test **Fertiliser** treatments.  
Plots are grouped into **Blocks**.

Treat **Block** as a random effect.

<div class="quiz-question">
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + Block</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (1|Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (Block|1)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (1 + Block)</div>
</div>



### Question 3

You measure **Yield**.  
You test **Fertiliser** treatments.  
Each **Block** contains multiple **Plots**.

Treat **Plot nested within Block** as random.

<div class="quiz-question">
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (1|Block/Plot)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (1|Plot/Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + (Plot|Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Yield ~ Fertiliser + Block/Plot</div>
</div>



### Question 4

You measure **Growth**.  
You test **Temperature**.  
You expect **Temperature effects to vary by Block**.

Fit a **random slope of Temperature within Block**.

<div class="quiz-question">
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Growth ~ Temp + (Temp|Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + (1|Temp/Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + (Block|Temp)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + Temp|Block</div>
</div>



### Question 5

You measure **Growth**.  
You test **Temperature**.  
You want both:

- Random intercepts for Block  
- Random slopes of Temperature within Block

<div class="quiz-question">
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + (Temp|1 + Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + (Temp + Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,true)">Growth ~ Temp + (1 + Temp|Block)</div>
<div class="quiz-option" onclick="checkAnswer(this,false)">Growth ~ Temp + (1|Temp + Block)</div>
</div>

:::

## Distributions {#sec-distributions}

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Distributions cheatsheet 
![](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BIOSCI738/cowstats/main/img/distributions.png)
:::

## Activities

### Activity 01

**As a group** *read* the cheatsheet above and summarize the following for each distribution:

 + What is the assumed random variable (i.e., variable being measured/modelled)
 + What form does this variable take (e.g., positive-only numbers, integer values etc.)
 + What are the parameters of each distribution and how do they relate to the mean?
 + Where does the encircled blue cow fall in terms of this herd's estimated population statistics?
 
Be prepared to answer questions as I wander around the class.

### Activity 02

Charlotte will soon allocate each group a distribution.

Using any means you wish read up about that particular distribution and note the following 

  1. Name of the distribution
  2. Parameter(s) of the distribution
  3. The mean and variance of the distribution in terms of the parameter(s)
  4. Support/characteristics of the distribution
  5. How parameter values change the distribution’s shape (hint use R to simulate the random variable using (a) parameter(s) of your choice and then plot this distribution)
  6. When might this distribution be used
  7. Answer the distribution-specific question(s)
  
**Your group reporter should make sure to be prepared to report to the class!**


::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Useful extras

Note: When exploring your distribution you may find this [RShiny application useful](https://cmjt.shinyapps.io/probable/) as well as the following cheat sheet

![The `p..()`, `d..()`, `q..()`, `r..()` functions in `R`](runsheets/img/pdqr.png)


:::


:::{.callout-warning icon=false}
## <i class="fa fa-question-circle" aria-hidden="true"></i> <i class="fa fa-users" aria-hidden="true"></i> <i class="fa fa-pencil-square" aria-hidden="true"></i> Modelling Charlotte's chickens

I have 11 chickens. Here is a nice picture hanging out in an area between their coop and the feeder.

<center><img src="runsheets/img/hens.JPG" height="400px" alt="chickens"></center>


Because I love stats (and chickens) I'm interested in modelling the number of chickens visible in this particular area at different times of the day. 

Q: What distribution should I use? **Tell me via [StatChat](https://canvas.auckland.ac.nz/courses/141056/external_tools/38296)** including your justification!
:::

## Introduction to Generalised Linear Models (GLMs) {#sec-glms}

**Slides**

<p align="center">
<iframe src="slides/06-01-intro_to_glms.pdf" height="400" width="700" title="Introduction to Generalised Linear Models"></iframe>
</p>


::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
##  Probability distributions quiz

Drag each distribution into the correct category.

<style>
.container {
  display: flex;
  gap: 25px;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
}

.box {
  border: 2px dashed #ccc;
  padding: 15px;
  width: 220px;
  min-height: 140px;
  border-radius: 8px;
}

.item {
  padding: 8px;
  margin: 5px;
  background: #f0f0f0;
  border-radius: 5px;
  cursor: grab;
}

.correct {
  background-color: #c8f7c5 !important;
}

h4 { margin-top: 0; }
</style>

<div class="container">

<div class="box" data-type="discrete">
<h4>Discrete</h4>
</div>

<div class="box" data-type="continuous">
<h4>Continuous</h4>
</div>

<div class="box" data-type="positive">
<h4>Positive Continuous</h4>
</div>

</div>

<br>

<div id="items">
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="discrete">Binomial</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="discrete">Poisson</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="discrete">Negative Binomial</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="continuous">Normal</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="continuous">Uniform</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="positive">Gamma</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="positive">Exponential</div>
<div class="item" draggable="true" data-type="positive">Lognormal</div>
</div>

<script>
const items = document.querySelectorAll('.item');
const boxes = document.querySelectorAll('.box');

items.forEach(item => {
  item.addEventListener('dragstart', dragStart);
});

boxes.forEach(box => {
  box.addEventListener('dragover', dragOver);
  box.addEventListener('drop', drop);
});

let dragged;

function dragStart() {
  dragged = this;
}

function dragOver(e) {
  e.preventDefault();
}

function drop() {
  if (dragged.dataset.type === this.dataset.type) {
    dragged.classList.add("correct");
  }
  this.appendChild(dragged);
}
</script>

:::



::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Which Distribution Would You Use? 

Drag each *variable* to the most appropriate distribution.

<style>
.container4 {
  display: flex;
  gap: 20px;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
}

.box4 {
  border: 2px dashed #ccc;
  padding: 15px;
  width: 220px;
  min-height: 170px;
  border-radius: 8px;
}

.item4 {
  padding: 8px;
  margin: 5px;
  background: #f0f0f0;
  border-radius: 5px;
  cursor: grab;
}

.correct {
  background-color: #c8f7c5 !important;
}
</style>

<div class="container4">

<div class="box4" data-type="normal">
<h4>Normal</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="poisson">
<h4>Poisson</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="negbin">
<h4>Negative Binomial</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="gamma">
<h4>Gamma</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="lognormal">
<h4>Lognormal</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="binomial">
<h4>Binomial</h4>
</div>

<div class="box4" data-type="beta">
<h4>Beta</h4>
</div>

</div>

<br>

<div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="poisson">
Number of insects per leaf (low counts, roughly equal mean and variance)
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="negbin">
Number of insects per plant (many zeros, high variability)
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="normal">
Measurement error in instrument readings
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="gamma">
Time until equipment failure
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="lognormal">
Body mass measurements (right-skewed)
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="binomial">
Number of infected plants out of 20 tested
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="beta">
Proportion of leaf area damaged
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="gamma">
Waiting time between arrivals
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="lognormal">
Income data (strong right skew)
</div>

<div class="item4" draggable="true" data-type="beta">
Survival probability estimates
</div>

</div>

<script>
const items4 = document.querySelectorAll('.item4');
const boxes4 = document.querySelectorAll('.box4');

items4.forEach(item => {
  item.addEventListener('dragstart', dragStart4);
});

boxes4.forEach(box => {
  box.addEventListener('dragover', dragOver4);
  box.addEventListener('drop', drop4);
});

let dragged4;

function dragStart4() {
  dragged4 = this;
}

function dragOver4(e) {
  e.preventDefault();
}

function drop4() {
  if (dragged4.dataset.type === this.dataset.type) {
    dragged4.classList.add("correct");
  }
  this.appendChild(dragged4);
}
</script>
:::
